
⏺ Sure.

  A lighthouse keeper named Odette lived alone on a rock that the sea had been trying to swallow for two hundred years. Every night she climbed the spiral stairs, lit the lamp, and watched the dark water for
  ships that almost never came.

  One winter, a bottle washed up on the shore. Inside was a letter, in handwriting she didn't recognize, that read: Thank you for the light last Tuesday. I would not have made it home. No signature. No ship's
  name.

  Odette had not lit the lamp the previous Tuesday. She'd been sick in bed, the tower dark, the wick cold.

  She kept the letter in her pocket for the rest of her life, and every Tuesday after that she climbed the stairs early, and lit the lamp twice — once for the sailors she could see, and once for whoever, or
  whatever, had been keeping watch when she couldn't.

✻ Sautéed for 8s

❯ another please

⏺ There was a clockmaker in a small mountain town who built a clock with thirteen hours.

  He hid the thirteenth hour between eleven and midnight, in a notch of brass no bigger than a fingernail. Nobody noticed. The clock kept time like any other — chimed at noon, chimed at six, the baker still
  opened at five — but once a day, for sixty unaccounted minutes, the town slipped sideways into a quiet that nobody remembered afterward.

  In that hour, the clockmaker did the things he could not do in the other twelve. He wrote letters to his dead wife. He left bread on the doorstep of the widow two streets over, who never figured out where it
  came from. He sat on the church steps and cried without anyone seeing.

  When he died, his apprentice opened the clock to clean it and found the thirteenth gear. She understood immediately. She did not remove it.

  Some say the clock is still running. Some say that's why, in certain mountain towns, you sometimes feel for a moment that the day was a little longer than it should have been — and that something kind happened
  in it, though you can't quite say what.

✻ Worked for 10s

❯ and another?

⏺ A girl named Wren found a door in the back of her grandmother's wardrobe, which would be unremarkable except that the door was only four inches tall.

  She knelt and put her eye to it. On the other side was a whole country — green hills, a river, a town with smoke curling from its chimneys — all of it tiny and perfect and going about its business without her.

  She visited the door every day for a summer. She learned the rhythms of the little country: market day, the fishing boats coming in, a wedding once with bells so small they sounded like rain on a windowpane.
  She never tried to force the door open. It seemed rude.

  On the last day of August, she pressed a folded note against the gap at the bottom — I see you. I hope you are well. — and slid it through.

  The next morning a note came back. It was the size of a moth's wing, and she had to use her grandmother's reading glass to read it. It said: We have seen you too, all summer, watching. We were not sure if you
  were a god or a window. Thank you for being kind either way.

  Wren is grown now, with children of her own. The wardrobe is in her attic. Sometimes, late at night, she goes up and kneels by the little door, and slides through a note about how the world is going — the wars,
   the weather, the small good things — because it seems only fair, after all those years of watching, to keep them in the loop.

✻ Churned for 11s

────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

  ? for shortcuts
